titleStmt.title: Mesopourous Fe3O4@SiO2-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite: Green sonochemical synthesis using strawberry fruit extract as a capping agent, characterization and their application in sulfasalazine delivery and cytotoxicity

analytic.title: Mesopourous Fe3O4@SiO2-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite: Green sonochemical synthesis using strawberry fruit extract as a capping agent, characterization and their application in sulfasalazine delivery and cytotoxicity

biblStruct.note: Received 15 February 2020; Received in revised form 25 April 2020; Accepted 3 June 2020

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: 0c5e0cf

startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=1, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=true, includeRawCitations=true, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], sentenceSegmentation=false, flavor=null

textClass.keywords: Sulfasalazine delivery Sonochemistry XTT Nanocomposite Magnetic carrier Hydroxyapatite

div.p: The present study introduces a simple, biocompatible and effective drug delivery system by using mesoporous nanocomposite-based platform. To achieve this goal, mesopourous Fe 3 O 4 @SiO 2 -hydroxyapatite nanocomposite (mFSH) was synthesized by sonochemical process in presence of strawberry fruit extract as capping agent (mFSH-SW). The impact of various factors such as sonication time (5, 15, 30 and 45 min), capping agent (cherry (CH), strawberry (SW), malus domestica (MD), andean blackberry (AB)), pH (10, 11 and 12) and sonication power (30, 60 and 80 W) were investigated to reach optimum condition. To reach high efficiency of drug loading, mFSH was grafted with 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES). Uniform, regular and spherical morphology of nanocomposite were specified by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), high-resolution transmission electron

div.head: Introduction
div.p: In recent years, the sonochemical method which applied for synthesis of various materials with different shape in nano size has been an efficient synthesis route. Sonochemical reactions have been utilized as the eco-friendly, rapid and facile process for multiple tasks including nanoclusters industrial and synthesis of multifunctional nanomaterial. Many attempts were made to apply eco-friendly and green process for the synthesis of nano dimension materials (Balachandramohan et al., 2020;Wu et al., 2018;Wang et al., 2020). These attempts include the utilization of natural extracts (like fruit and plant) as capping agent, reducing agent and stabilizer to control on size and morphology of products. Therefore, appropriate products (suitable size and morphology) can be obtained by using the sonochemical method and using the fruit extract as capping agents. In this research, we used a cost effective, facile and green procedure to synthesis of mFSH nanocomposite using cherry, strawberry, malus domestica, andean blackberry (with particular features like environment compatibility, higher biodegradability and lower toxicity) for the first time. Nanomaterials (such as magnetic materials) are used in various fields such as catalysts, pharmaceutical water treatment and medical (Khojasteh et al., 2019;Kumbhakar et al., 2018;Mortazavi-Derazkola et al., 2020;Yue et al., 2020;Ardestani et al., 2020). Drug delivery is an encouraging therapeutic method for treating disease; nonetheless effective delivery of drugs to targets with negligible side effects has remained a challenge. Up to now, numerous research have been made to fabrication novel nanomaterials and exploration of their application in various therapeutic fields (Zhu et al., 2005). Side effects reduction, followed by reduction of efficient drug dosage, is main advantage of novel drug delivery systems (Sudimack and Lee, 2000;Yokoyama, 2005). So, targeted drug delivery and control release are an efficient way to control drug carrier platform (Slowing et al., 2007). Wide range of materials have been utilized in drug delivery systems such as calcium phosphate cement (del Real et al., 2002;Bohner, 2000), SBA-15 (Moritz and łaniecki, 2011), zeolites (Vilaça et al., 2013) etc. (Choi et al., 2018;Nikolaou and Krasia-Christoforou, 2018;Deepak et al., 2018), but it should be considered that overdose of these materials may increase the possibility of side effects. Mesoporous ceramics as a bioactive material have revealed great ability as sensor (Amiri et al., 2017a) dye removal (Amiri et al., 2017b) and delivery of drugs application (Amiri et al., 2017c). Due to particle size of the mesoporous silica, these materials are able to be endocytosed properly (He et al., 2009) and they can be transmitted totally as a flawless transporter in controlled drug delivery systems (Lu et al., 2009).

Hydroxyapatite (Ca 5 (PO 4 ) 3 (OH)) with biodegradable and biocompatible properties is one of the most basic materials which has been in ceramic compound (Bezzi et al., 2003;Suchanek and Yoshimura, 2011). Lately, numerous investigations have been done on drug delivery systems such as atenolol (Bhattacharjee et al., 2017), gentamicin (Golda-Cepa et al., 2018), ibuprofen (Pawlik et al., 2017) and other drugs (Yu et al., 2018;González-Villegas et al., 2017). Magnetic materials are unique materials which can be managed with external magnetic field and used in diverse field of pharmacy and medicine. Ferrites, metal oxide and pure metals used to synthesize magnetic materials (Faraji et al., 2010;Mahmoudi et al., 2011). Magnetic nanoparticles are applied as drug carriers which can be accumulated in a target tissue. The concept is commonly referred to as magnetic drug targeting (MDT) (Amiri et al., 2018).

Long-term toxic effects are one of the main concerns about nanoparticles. To overcome this concern, magnetic materials were covered with HAp. Furthermore, the large pores and high specific surface area of magnetic nanocomposite-hydroxyapatite enable it to be utilized in drug delivery systems. Sulfasalazine (SSN, C 18 H 14 N 4 O 5 S) as an antiinflammatory issue commonly used to treat autoimmune disease. SSN drug is changed to sulfapyridine by bacteria in the gut (Fan et al., 2011). Side effects appear when the level of serum reach above 50 μg/L. Rarely, sulfasalazine creates depression and anxiety besides its therapeutic effects in young males. Most of the chemotherapeutic treatments represent unwanted results on healthy cells because non-precise and inadequate targeted drug delivery occurs (Chomoucka et al., 2010;Blanco et al., 2015). Various research on sulfasalazine delivery (Popova et al., 2018;Puoci et al., 2004;Timin et al., 2015;Olaitan and Chaw, 2019;Ghasemi et al., 2017;Lee et al., 2008;Popat et al., 2014) have been listed in Table 1. On the basis of the information above mentioned, facile, cost effective and green synthesis of magnetic nanocomposite as a sulfasalazine carrier are quite interesting. Up to now, there is no related report along with (sulfasalazine delivery with mFSH magnetic nanocomposite).

The purpose of the research was to introduce a novel sonochemical process for the synthesis of mFSH-SW nanocomposite and scrutinize the effect of diverse green capping agents on the uniformity and size of obtained products. Also, another goal was to modify mFSH-SW nanocomposite with APTES (mFSH-SW@APTES) and investigate the ability of mFSH-SW@APTES as a novel nano-carrier for sulfasalazine delivery.

div.head: Synthesis of magnetic nanoparticles (FeNPs)
div.p: The magnetite nanoparticles fabricated through a co-precipitation route according to available literatures (Mortazavi-Derazkola et al., 2017a;Mortazavi-Derazkola et al., 2017b). Briefly, 1.85 mmol of FeCl 2 .4H 2 O and 3.75 mmol of FeCL 3 .6H 2 O (with the molar ratio of 1:2) dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water and stirred variously for 0.5 h under nitrogen atmosphere and room temperature. Subsequently, 300 mL of NH 4 OH was quickly added to the above solution. The black magnetic particles collected and washed for three periods (with ethanol and H 2 O) and in following the samples were dehydrated under ambient conditions for 24 h.

div.head: Synthesis of Fe-SiO 2 nanoparticles (FSNPs)
div.p: The interlayers of SiO 2 nanoparticles were prepared through the modified precipitation route. At first, 0.1 g of FeNPs was dispersed in 200 mL of solution containing 180 mL of ethanol and 20 mL of NH 4 OH (25 %). Afterwards, 1.2 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) slowly dropped in magnetic solution media with vigorous mixing for 24 h. FSNPs was obtained after centrifuged, washed and dried under vacuum at 70 for 5 h.

div.head: Preparation of fruit extract
div.p: The fresh strawberry fruit were collected and washed with distilled water completely to eliminate any medium ingredients from the biomass. It was dried at room temperature for 48 h. Next, 20 g of strawberry fruit extracted via the maceration process utilizing 150 mL of CH 3 OH solution (as extraction solvent) at room temperature for 2 days. After this time, the extract solution was filtered and evaporated utilizing a rotary device (Hei zbad WB eoc, Germany) to yield the extract. The final yield was applied as a green capping agent for fabrication of mFSH magnetic nanocomposites. The extraction of other fruit extracts was similar to this method.

div.head: Biosynthesis synthesis of mFS-hydroxyapatite nanocomposite (m-FSH)
div.p: The mFSH magnetic nanocomposite was fabricated via a simple sonochemical method. In a typical experiment, 10 mL of strawberry fruit extract was added gradually to 1.67 mmol of calcium nitrate solution (30 mL) under ultrasonic wave (60 W) and the mixture added slowly into the FSNPs solution. The appropriate amount of NH 3 was added gradually to the above solution until the pH value adjusted to 11. As a final point, 1 mmol of (NH 4 ) 2 HPO 4 was dispersed in 30 mL deionized water and added into the stirring mixture. After 24 h, the magnetic precipitates were centrifuged, washed and dried (70 for 8 h) and finally calcined at 500 for 2 h (formation of mFSH-SW). The reaction conditions for the preparation of magnetic products have been presented in Table . 2. Various fruits extracts, pH and sonication power and time on the size and shape of the products were studied. As well as, the blank sample was fabricated in the absence of extracts and ultrasonic irradiation.

div.head: Modification of nanocomposite by APTES (mFSH-SW@APTES)
div.p: In order to modify the sample surface, the modification of the mFSH-SW with amino groups was performed by the reaction of 3 mL of 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane and 150 mg of mFSH nanocomposite under dynamic mixing for 24 h. The as-obtained samples were washed (with ethanol and double distilled water) and dried under ambient conditions.

div.head: SLN loading and in vitro release
div.p: Drug loading into magnetic carrier was carried out with proportion of 1:1. Briefly, 150 mg of SLN was dispersed in 10 mL of NaOH (0.1 M) solution. Then, 150 mg of mFSH-SW magnetic nanocomposite was added slowly into the solution and vigorous stirred under ambient conditions for 24 h. The UV-vis analysis was used in order to decide the amount of SLN loaded. The adsorption band of sulfasalazine was determined at a maximum wavelength (359 nm).

For drug release study of magnetic carriers, the dialysis bag method at 37 and pH = 6.8 (intestinal environment) was used. 100 mg of SLN-loaded mFSH-SW carrier added to 100 mL of SBF solution and one milliliter of the solution was withdrawn at various times and 1 mL of SBF was replaced. The released SLN concentration was specified by UV/ Vis spectrophotometer at 359 nm (Scheme 1).

div.head: XTT cell proliferation assay
div.p: XTT is established on the decrease of yellow color tetrazolium salt (XTT) in active cells metabolically to dark colored formazan dye (Bancos et al., 2012). After HEK-293 cell line incubated (24 h and 48 h) using SLN-loading mFSH-SW@APTES (sample no. 2) and free drug, XTT reagent (50 μL) and PMS (1 μL) was added every well (every well contains 10,000 cells) at different concentration (0.1, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 mg/mL). Untreated cells were chosen as control (blank) group. The

div.head: R E T R A C T E D
div.p: plates were placed in the incubator at 5 % CO 2 and 37 °C. Optical density (OD) then read at 450 nm by ELISA reader.

div.head: In vitro apoptosis/necrosis assay
div.p: Apoptosis/necrosis quantification was carried out by the Annexin V-propidium iodide staining kit (Roche). The supernatant was collected after 48 h incubation of HEK-293 cells with SLN-loaded mFSH-SW@APTES (100 mg/mL) and the adherent cells (∼2 × 10 6 cells) were trypsinized. The cells collected, washed two times and finally centrifuged. After 15 min incubation of 10 6 cells with AnnexinV-PI reagent buffer at room temperature, samples were analyzed with flow cytometry.

div.head: In vivo toxicity study
div.p: Three rats (230 g) were purchased from pasture institute of IRAN. SLN-loaded mFSH-SW@APTES was injected intravenously through tail vein at a dosage of 1 mg/ml PBS for 48 h. Three rats were chosen as control group. The rats were dissected out and to more examinations spleen tissues, liver, and kidney. These were fixed with formalin (10 %) and alcohol (70, 80, 90, 95 and 100 %) was applied for dehydration. After washing the tissues, the tissues were entrenched in paraffin for another experimental (hematoxylin and eosin).

div.head: Characterization techniques
div.p: The crystallinity and phase of the as-obtained products was investigated using X-ray diffraction pattern (Philips PW 1800 using Cu Kα radiation). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the powders acquired using Shimadzu 460 spectrometer. The UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded on an Ultraviolet-Visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu; UV-3101PC; Japan). The surface morphology of samples was performed using SEM (LEO-1455VP) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images utilizing JEM-2100. The purity of products was determined by energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDS; XL30, Philips). The magnetization curves were detected at room ambient under varying field of -10,000 to 10,000 Oe (Meghnatis Kavir Kashan Co., Iran).

div.head: Phase purity analysis
div.p: Fig. 1 illustrates the wide-angle XRD patterns of FeNPs (a) and mFSH-SW nanocomposite (b). The intense diffraction peaks at 2 = 18.6 °, 30.5 °, 35.9 °, 43.5 °, 54 °, 57.6 °and 63.2 °show the cubic structure of magnetic nanoparticles which are consistent with the JCPDS data (JCPDS card No. 75-0449). The XRD pattern of mesoporous nanocomposite (sample no. 2) that was synthesized in the presence of strawberry and ultrasonic waves (60 W) is exposed in Fig. 1b. The XRD patterns indicates that crystalline FeNPs and mFSH-SW nanocomposite have been synthesized correctly and also there were no notable impurities in FeNPs and sample no. 2.

div.head: Structure analysis
div.p: FT-IR spectrophotometers were used to confirm the functional group of products. The FT-IR spectrum of the pure magnetite (a), FSNPs (b), mFSH-SW (c) and mFSH-SW@APTES nanocomposite (d) are presented in Fig. 2. As can be seen in Fig. 2, the peaks at around 3420 and 1640 cm -1 are assigned to the hydroxyl bending vibration and stretching modes, respectively (Safari- Amiri et al., 2017;Mortazavi-Derazkola et al., 2016). In Fig. 2a, the sharp absorption band located at Scheme 1. Schematic illustration of the preparation of mFSH-SW@APTES and SSN-loaded nanocomposite.

div.head: R E T R A C T E D
div.p: 570 cm -1 can be attributed to FeeO vibration stretching of FeNPs. According to Fig. 2b, Si-O-Si stretching band at about 1080 cm -1 is obviously observed. Moreover, absorption bands at 587, 486 and 961 cm -1 are assigned to the SieOeFe, OeSieO and SieO, respectively (Schraml-Marth et al., 1992). The FT-IR spectrum of mFSH-SW nanocomposite (sample no. 2) is shown in Fig. 2c. The peaks at 486, 566 and 580 cm -1 could be related to PO 4 3-groups. A sharp band at 1046 cm -1 was assigned to the vibration stretching of the phosphate group (Tian et al., 2014;Yang et al., 2010). In the curve (d), the expanded spectra with wavenumber range of 1526 and 2900 cm -1 could be assigned to the amino and propyl groups, respectively. Therefore, the results of FT-IR spectra confirmed that APTES groups modified on the surface of the mFSH-SW (sample no. 2).

div.head: Morphological-elemental characteristics
div.p: FESEM images of as-obtained samples (FeNPs, FSNPs and mFSH magnetic product) are presented in Fig. S1. In Fig. S1(a), as-obtained magnetic FeNPs have a spherical shape and regular structures with particle size of about 38 nm. According to Fig. S1(b), the particle size of FSNPs were increased (approximately 50 nm). This growth demonstrated that the SiO 2 layers were coated successfully on the surface of Fe 3 O 4 nanoparticles. Sonication power, sonication time, pH and capping agents are four significant parameters that influence on the shape and size of products. For further study of sonication power influence on the shape and size of the products, experiments were performed at different power: 30, 60 and 80 W (Fig. S1(c-e)). As it is clear in Fig. S1(c), high agglomeration and large size were characteristic of sample no. 1. It seems that spherical sample no. 2 divided into smaller particles by increasing sonication power to 60 W (Fig. S1(d)). Regular and uniform structures with spherical morphology were great features of these products. Due to the Fig. S1(e), products with 100-140 nm were prepared under high sonication power (80 W; 24 KHz; sample no. 3). Slight agglomeration and medium size are features of this sample. In the ultrasonic process, bubbles are formed that lead to release of high energy and temperature after decomposition (pressure of approximately 1800 atm, temperature of approximately 5000 K). This process can provide the energy need to formation of magnetic nanocomposite. So, optimum power for preparation of regular and homogeneous uniform magnetic nanocomposite is 60 W. Because reaction time in sonochemical process is directly related with morphology and size of products, so its effect was studied. The experiment was carried out at various time: 5, 15, 30 and 45 min. Fig. S1(f) and Fig. S2(a, b) illustrate FESEM images of as-synthesized sample no 4-6 under different sonication time. By increasing ultrasonic time from 5 up to 30 min, morphology and size of final products changed. The FESEM micrograph shows that by increasing reaction time to 30 min, particle size of product reduced gradually. In contrast, by increasing the sonication time to 45 min, the particles size increased (Fig. S2(b)). These phenomena are attributed to Ostwald mechanism. Owing to the Ostwald process, the larger particles grow at the expense of the smaller particles, diameters of magnetic products increased with increasing sonication time from 5 min to 30 min. Therefore, obtained results showed that optimum time for fabrication of well and uniform nanocomposite is 30 min. Fig. S2(c and d) show the effect of pH (10 and 12) on the shape and size of products (sample no. 7 and 8). Micro/nanostructure, agglomerated structures and large size are features of structures that were prepared at pH 10 and pH 12. To examine the fruits extract effect, used in the role of green capping agents on the particle grain size and shape of the products, mFSH product was prepared by cherry (sample no. 9), malus domestica (sample no. 10), Andean blackberry (sample no. 11) and strawberry (sample no. 2) after sonication (60 W) for 30 min. FESEM images of these products are illustrated in Fig. S2(e-f) and Fig. S3(a). The results displayed that using cherry and malus domestica as capping agent cause of highly agglomerated and heterogeneous products (sample no. 9 and 10). Afterwards, morphology of as-obtained product using andean blackberry (sample no. 11; Fig. S3(a)) was studied. Low agglomeration and particles size about 90-110 nm are unique of this product (sample no. 11). By comparing FESEM images, particles size of the mFSH prepared by strawberry (sample no. 2) are homogenous spherical, regular and smaller than those produced by other capping agents (about 85 nm). Strawberry fruit extract indicated the most promising role for controlling the morphology and particle size of mFSH samples (small size, regular and homogeneous spherical shape). For determining of the influence of the capping agent and ultrasonic irradiation on the particle size and shape of samples, the blank (control) test was accomplished in the absence of any capping factor (sample no. 12) and sonication (sample no. 13). Fig. S3(b and c) shows the FESEM images of the asobtained sample (gained from the blank test). It is clearly obvious that the shape and size of the products are very irregular microstructures, large and the chance of aggregation between particles was improved. These results illustrated that the presence of fruits extract as a capping agent and ultrasonic irradiation are very favorable to produce uniform nanocomposite.

EDS analysis was applied to explore the surface structure of products and distribution of local elements. Fig. S4(a) demonstrates the EDS spectrum of the mFSH-SW nanocomposite (sample no. 2). Due to the result, the presence of elements including Fe, P, Si, O and Ca are confirmed in this analysis. Consequently, both EDS and XRD results confirmed that nanocomposite effectively prepared by the current method. DLS technique was used to determine the size distribution profile of sample no. 2 (Fig. S4(b)). DLS data show that green synthesized nanocomposite size distribution range between approximately 70-110 nm which corresponded well with the FESEM images.

The morphology and accurate size of products (sample no. 2, 12 and 13) were specified by TEM technique (Fig. S5). As exposed in Fig. S5(a and b), large particle size, high agglomeration and amorphous morphology are characteristics of samples no 12 and 13. TEM images (Fig. S5(c-e)) displayed that spherical product (sample no. 2) with diameter of about 80 nm were prepared. The results validated that the finally magnetic products have a similar core-shell structure with dark contrast core of magnetic particles and light contrast HAp shell. Moreover, the distinction and resolution in TEM technique are much better than FESEM images. Accordingly, the FESEM images (Fig. S1(d)) designated that the size of particles were significantly larger than the TEM images particle size. Fig. S5(f) illustrates the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of sample no. 2 wherein, the incorporated HAp on the surface of the Fe 3 O 4 nanoparticles can be seen.

div.head: R E T R A C T E D
div.p: Due to the image, the distance between the interlayers were 0.26 nm and 0.34 nm for Fe 3 O 4 (311) and hydroxyapatite (002), respectively, which were in agreement with the XRD data. So, it is obvious that magnetic nanocomposite were successfully prepared by using strawberry fruits extracts and sonochemical process.

div.head: Magnetic-porosity investigation
div.p: The magnetic properties of the pure magnetite, FSNPs and mFSH-SW nanocomposite (sample no. 2) were determined by VSM analysis at room temperature. As represented in Fig. 3a, FeNPs, FSNPs and sample no. 2 have magnetization saturation (Ms) values of 69.2, 46.7 and 19.8 emu/g, respectively. After coating with SiO 2 and HAp, the saturation magnetization of Fe 3 O 4 decreased evidently. Also, the VSM analysis demonstrated that all samples have superparamagnetism features to be separable when magnet applied. The N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of the mFSH-SW are shown in Fig. 3b. Through the hysteresis loops cataloguing given by IUPAC, the as-obtained magnetic product curve exhibited a type IV isotherm (hysteresis loop of H3) (Lai et al., 2016). Porous structure of magnetic nanocomposite is one of the interesting parameters which is determining absorption and release profile of sulfasalazine. This means that pore volume, surface area, pore size of products have dramatic effect on the drug delivery performance. Therefore, the characteristics of magnetic nanocomposite were studied using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Brunauer-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) techniques. According to BET and BJH analysis, average pore size, pore volume and surface area of mFSH-SW were 14.1 nm, 0.24 cm 3 g -1 and 63.2 m 2 g -1 , respectively.

div.head: Sonication influence and preparation mechanism
div.p: There are three steps in the sonochemical process: formation of bubble, growth and collapse of bubbles (Kumar et al., 2000). Interim temperature and bubbles pressure provide promising situation for crystals growth in nano size range. The explosion of bubbles generates a shake wave that allocates to the reaction surroundings. In addition, bubble explosion can interject the bubble circular symmetry by microjets preparation that effected on the surface. Therefore, implementation in remarkable ability changes in combination, morphology, and reactivity that happened by the interparticle encounter (Hinman and Suslick, 2017;Prozorov et al., 2004). The probable mechanism of mFSH-SW nanocomposite preparation by sonochemical method can be considered as follows (Eqs. ( 1)-( 9)):
H OH Ultrasonic irradiation . .
+ OH OH H2O2

) + OH Other samples Oxidized samples . (4) + + + + + NH3 H2 O H NH4 OH samples . ((5)) + + Ca(NO3)2 OH Ca(OH)2 NO3 . (6) (NH4)2HPO4 HPO42 (7) + + Ca(OH)2 HPO42 CaHPO4 H2O (8) + CaHPO4 H2 O Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2

Uniform, homogeneous and spherical morphology of magnetic nanocomposite are prepared in presence of ultrasonic irradiations. The H and OH radicals appeared after pyrolysis of water. Then, process continued with production of hydrogen peroxide (H 2 O 2 ). Cavity formation is one of the significant roles in preparation of fine nanocomposite in sonochemical process (high pressures of about 1800 atm and temperatures of about 5000 K). After the bubbles formed, these bubbles were erupted and destroyed due to the shake waves (hot-spot theory). Released energy can help to formation of Ca(OH) 2 and HPO 4 2-. This phenomenon can create smaller nanoparticles through impact on the particle levels and the energy generated of bubbles degradation. Finally, valuable modifying in the shape, grain size and structure of products were occurred by the interparticle collisions. Overall, ultrasonic irradiation is one of the principal factors for preparation of sample no. 2.

div.head: Drug loading and release mesurment
div.p: The percentage of sulfasalazine loaded in mFSH (sample no. 2; without APTES) and mFSH@APTES (sample no. 11, 12 and 2) were determined by UV-vis spectra. All experiments repeated at least three times. The UV-vis results showed that entrapment efficiency for pure mFSH, mFSH@APTES (sample no. 11, 12 and 2) were calculated as 28.4 %, 49.3 %, 35.7 %, and 59.1 % respectively. Entrapment Efficiency (EE) calculated by following equation:
Where A stays, the primary added drug and B is the unentrapped drug.

Samples were collected at specified times and released amount of the sulfasalazine was determined by UV/Vis analysis (at 359 nm). Briefly, 5 mL of sample was taken and then the released amount of sulfasalazine evaluated by a calibration curve. The drug release percentage calculated by the following equation: R E T R A C T E D = Percentage release of drug (%) Released sulfasalazine at definite time Amount of sulfasalazine loaded into the nanocomposite * 100 (11)

The SLN release pattern from pure FSH, mFSH@APTES (sample no. 11, 12 and 2) at pH = 6.8 (simulated intestinal environment) were carried out and shown in Fig. 4. High agglomeration particles of sample no. 12, relatively uniform morphologies of sample no. 11 and very homogeneous spherical particles of sample no. 2 that prepared by applying novel green capping agent and ultrasonic irradiation was employed for release of SLN and their drug delivery performance compared at 36 h.

Generally, the limits of existing systems of drug delivery contain limited effective targeting, suboptimal bioavailability and potential cytotoxicity. In pH = 6.8, SLN loaded pure FSH shows a rapid release of the drug up to 42.9 % at the beginning. In addition, the SLN released from mFSH@APTES (sample no. 11, 12 and 2) shows up to ca. 22.1, 16.3 and 26.4 % at the beginning. Generally, major limitations in drug delivery systems include: restrictions ineffective targeting, suboptimal bioavailability and potential cytotoxicity. In pH = 6.8, SLN loaded pure FSH showed rapid release of drug up to 42.9 % at the beginning. In addition, the SLN released from mFSH@APTES (sample no. 11, 12 and 2) showed up to ca. 22.1, 16.3 and 26.4 % at the beginning. The possible mechanism of the initial release may be assumed as: the drug molecules existing nearby the superficial were released swiftly to the solution, while the molecules captured in the pore need more time to escape from the pores. Conferring to Fig. 4, the efficiency of the sulfasalazine release in the intestine pH-similar milieu after 36 h were 81.4 %, 51.2 % and 100 % for mFSH@APTES (sample no. 11, 12 and 2). Sulfasalazine was released faster from pure FSH, followed by sample no. 12, sample no. 11 and sample no. 2. This can be due to the weaker interaction among the OH group of sulfasalazine and the amino group of APTES-modified nanocomposite (sample no. 2). Sulfasalazine released slower than other samples (after 36 h) considering morphology, size and high amount number of amino groups in the nanocomposite (sample no. 2). In contrast, the whole SLN molecules released when APTES was not used as a linker with sulfasalazine (at 12 h). It is obvious that the grain size, shape and appropriate distribution of the pore have an effective impact on the drug release.

div.head: MTT assay
div.p: The result of the in-vitro cytotoxicity assay is shown in Fig. 5. At 24 and 48 h of incubation, SLN displayed cytotoxic effect at (100 mg/mL) and (50 and 100 mg/mL), but none of all concentrations of SLN-loaded mFSH-SW@APTES at both time were meaningfully cytotoxic. This result was obtained due to the biodegradability of nanocomposites. Apoptosis/necrosis assay showed that at the endpoint of the HEK-293 cell line treatment at nontoxic concentration (100 mg/mL) the amount of necrosis and apoptosis were low (Fig. 6). Moreover, data obtained non-toxic properties of SLN-loaded mFSH-SW@APTES on HEK-293 cell line after 48 h.

div.head: R E T R A C T E D
Histopathology studies
div.p: Histopathological study of kidney, liver and spleen (a, c, e) before SSN-loaded mFSH-SW@APTES injection (control group) and (b, d, f) after SLN-loaded mFSH-SW@APTES injection were exhibited in Fig. 7. Any mortality was not detected during the 48 h of the study. No inflammatory changes were seen for SLN-loaded mFSH-SW@APTES and control groups in spleen and liver tissue. Also was not any necrotic tubule abnormality in kidney tissue. Obtained results showed that asprepared nanocarriers can be applied in various medical fields.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: In summary, mFSH-SW nanocomposites were prepared through a quick and simplistic sonochemical route. In this route, fruits extract were applied as a capping agent for the synthesis of the mesoporous nanocomposite. Size, morphology and crystal structures of the products were investigated by FT-IR, XRD, SEM, EDS, HRTEM, VSM, DLS and N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherm. Also, nanocomposite was used to study sulfasalazine release behavior at pH = 6.8. The results displayed that fruits extract as a capping agent have intense impact on the morphology and pore volume determination of mFSH-SW nanocomposite. Results of the study indicated that the as-prepared nanocomposite can be used as effective carrier to drug delivery systems. Therefore drug-loaded magnetic carrier indicated the reduce time of administration and minimization of the dose and side effects.

figure.head: Fig. 1 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 1. XRD pattern of (a) FeNPs and (b) mFSH nanocomposite (sample no. 2).

figure.head: Fig. 2 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 2. FT-IR spectrum of (a) FeNPs, (b) FSNPs, (c) mFSH-SW nanocomposite and (d) mFSH-SW@APTES nanocomposites.

figure.head: Fig. 3 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 3. Room temperature magnetization curve of samples (a) and nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm of the mFSH-SW nanocomposite (b).

figure.head: Fig. 4 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 4. Sulfasalazine delivery of samples synthesized at different conditions, (a) without APTES, (b) mFSH-SW nanocomposite, (c) mFSH-AB nanocomposite and (d) product without capping agent.

figure.head: Fig. 5 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 5. XTT assay: HEK-293 cells were exposed to the SSN (a) and (b) SSNloaded mFSH-SW@APTES nanocomposite.

figure.head: Fig. 6 .Fig. 7 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 6. Apoptosis/necrosis assay plots of HEK-293 cells treated with 100 mg/ml of the samples after 48 h of incubation time: (a) normal (untreated) and (b) cells treated with SSN-loaded mFSH-SW@APTES nanocomposite.R E T R A C T E D

figure.head: Table 1
figure.figDesc: Summary table of previous research studies.

row.cell: E T R A C T E D

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: The authors hereby express their thanks for the support rendered by Nanjing Forestry University [Grant Nos. 163020139 and 163020210], Science and Technology Department of Jiangsu Province (BK2020043008) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (5201101466). Authors are grateful to the Council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant (159271/001).

div.head: Declaration of Competing Interest
div.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

div.head: Appendix A. Supplementary data
div.p: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.123140.
